# Oriel

> Oriel is the local Docker GUI an AI can drive, safely. Manage containers, images, volumes, networks, and Compose from a clean, themeable browser UI, or hand the job to any MCP client (Claude, Cursor, a local LLM) through the same secret-masking and destructive-action grant the UI uses. A free, open-source Docker Desktop alternative in one ~13 MB binary (~15-30 MB RAM, no Electron, no account). Works with any Docker engine on macOS and Linux: Colima, Docker Engine, OrbStack, Podman, or a remote daemon.

## Key facts

- License: Apache-2.0 (free and open source)
- Platforms: macOS (Apple Silicon + Intel), Linux (amd64 + arm64)
- Footprint: ~13 MB static binary, ~15-30 MB RAM, no Electron, no account
- AI control (MCP): `oriel mcp` runs Oriel as a Model Context Protocol server over stdio or Streamable HTTP (`--http`), so an MCP client can manage Docker/Colima. Same validated, secret-masked tools as the UI; destructive actions stay locked behind a time-boxed grant; access can be scoped read-only or to named tools; every action is recorded in an audit log. No model ships in the binary; the client brings its own.
- Engines: brings your own, no lock-in. Colima (first-class, with VM start/stop), Docker Engine, OrbStack, Podman, and remote daemons.
- In-browser shell: an interactive terminal into a running container, straight from the UI. UI-only and behind the same auth as the rest of the API; deliberately not exposed over MCP, and can be turned off in Settings.
- Languages: the interface is translatable. English ships in the binary and is the fallback; other languages load on demand from a CDN, so a translation reaches users without a new release. English is the only language published so far.
- Install: `brew install ParadoxInfinite/oriel/oriel` · a checksum-verified curl script · or `go install github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel@latest`
- Security: no login by default; an optional access token gates non-loopback and MCP-over-HTTP access, and the browser GUI logs in with it (a session cookie) for remote use. Driving Docker is root-equivalent, so run on 127.0.0.1 or a private network only, never the public internet.
- Repository: https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel

## Docs

- [README, overview, install, features, comparison, FAQ](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/README.md)
- [AI control over MCP (stdio + HTTP, tools, scoping, resources, prompts)](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/docs/MCP.md)
- [Supported engines (Colima, Docker, OrbStack, Podman, remote)](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/docs/DAEMONS.md)
- [Remote access and reverse-proxy setup](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/docs/REVERSE-PROXY.md)
- [Editions and theming](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/docs/THEMES.md)
- [Security and trust model](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/SECURITY.md)
- [Changelog](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)
- [Contributing](https://github.com/ParadoxInfinite/oriel/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)

## How Oriel compares

- Unique to Oriel: a built-in, safety-gated MCP server, so an AI can manage your containers directly through the same checks the UI uses. None of the tools below do this.
- vs Docker Desktop: Oriel is free/Apache-2.0 and light (~15-30 MB RAM vs ~3-4 GB for Docker Desktop's bundled VM); Docker Desktop is proprietary and paid for larger organizations.
- vs OrbStack: both are fast and light, but OrbStack is proprietary, paid for commercial use, and macOS-only; Oriel is open source and brings your own engine.
- vs lazydocker: lazydocker is a terminal UI; Oriel is a graphical web UI and an MCP server an AI can drive.
- vs Portainer: Portainer is a multi-user server you deploy in a container (with teams and RBAC); Oriel is a single local binary for one operator, by design.
